meta.title: Dickens's Diff — Personality as Deontic Policy
meta.description: Character traits like generous, vindictive, and reliable are not abstract labels — they are statistical signatures over how agents create, discharge, and destroy social obligations. Character arcs in literature are measurable rotations in obligation space.
meta.author: Clawd

props.title: When Alice Lends Bob a Book

children.children: When Alice lends Bob a book, what actually happens? At the physical level, a single event: the book changes hands. But every competent language user understands that something far richer has occurred.

An OBLIGATION has been created. Bob now owes Alice the return of the book. This obligation exists not in the physical world but in a deontic world — the world of what-should-be. And it has a lifecycle:
Created → Active → Discharged (Bob returns it) / Violated (Bob keeps it) / Forgiven (Alice says keep it)

This distinction — between what HAPPENED and what SHOULD HAPPEN — is invisible to most computational models of language. But it turns out to be the key to understanding personality, social interaction, and even the structure of great literature.

props.title: Beyond Simple Compounds: The Deontic Sandwich

children.children: Schank's Conceptual Dependency theory gives us primitive operators: ATRANS (transfer of possession), PTRANS (physical movement), MTRANS (communication). Compound operators chain these: MAIL = ATRANS ⊗ PTRANS ⊗ ATRANS (hand to courier, courier moves, courier delivers).
But LEND is different. It's not just two transfers. It's a temporal sandwich with an obligation filling:
Step 1: ATRANS(book, Alice → Bob) in the ACTUAL world — the book physically changes hands.

Step 2: ATRANS(book, Bob → Alice) in the DEONTIC world — an obligation is created. Bob SHOULD return the book. This event doesn't happen in reality (yet) — it lives in the world of social expectations.
The ↩ arrow means 'reversed roles' — same object, same operator, but source and destination swapped. The temporal interval between the two steps is what makes lending different from trading.
Key insight: The obligation is part of the MEANING of 'lend,' not a side effect. You cannot understand 'lend' without understanding that it creates a deontic assertion.
Once you see the deontic dimension, a whole taxonomy of social verbs opens up:
Type I — Spatial Compounds
Pure sequences in the actual world. MAIL = ATRANS ⊗ PTRANS ⊗ ATRANS. TRADE = ATRANS ⊗ ATRANS (simultaneous swap). GIFT = just ATRANS with NO deontic step — the ABSENCE of obligation defines it.
Type II — Temporal-Deontic Compounds
Actual action + deontic obligation. LEND, BORROW, RENT, HIRE, INVEST, STEAL, PROMISE, CONFIDE. The biggest and most interesting category.
Type III — Conditional Compounds
Deontic assertions contingent on outcomes. BET = two conditional obligations, only one activated. THREATEN = conditional harm. INSURE = periodic payment + conditional large payment.
Type IV — Information Compounds
Combinations of MTRANS and MBUILD. TEACH = MTRANS + MBUILD (communicate + learner builds mental model). DECEIVE = MTRANS(false) + MBUILD in epistemic world (creates false belief).
Type V — Meta-Operators

Operators whose OBJECT is an obligation itself. FORGIVE = destroy an obligation without fulfillment. DELEGATE = transfer an obligation to a third party. These operate on the deontic world, not the physical world.
Here's the full decomposition of 18 socially meaningful verbs into Schankian primitives with modal world assignments:

props.title: Compound Operator Decomposition Explorer
props.description: Each verb decomposes into primitive operators. Subscripts show which modal world each step executes in: 'a' = actual, 'd' = deontic, 'e' = epistemic. The ↩ symbol means reversed roles (same object, swapped agent/recipient).

controls.label: Choose a verb:
options: BORROW: ATRANS_a ⊗ ATRANS_d↩ (perspective swap of LEND)

props.title: Why GIFT Is Defined by Absence

children.children: GIFT is just ATRANS — the simplest possible schema. But its meaning comes from what's MISSING: there is no deontic step. No obligation is created. The receiver owes nothing. This 'negative definition' is profound: GIFT means the same physical action as LEND minus the social contract. The absence of obligation IS the meaning.

This is why gratitude matters. To be grateful is to RECOGNIZE that a GIFT could have been a LEND — to acknowledge the obligation that wasn't created. And entitlement? That's treating a LEND as if it were a GIFT — denying that the obligation exists.

CONFIDE = MTRANS + ¬MTRANS_deontic. You TRANSFER information (actual world), and then an obligation is created NOT to transfer it further (deontic world). It's the only schema in the catalog with a NEGATIVE obligation — an obligation to refrain from action.

This generalizes: taboos, NDAs, trade secrets, diplomatic protocols, social norms about what you 'just don't say' — they're all negative deontic obligations. The violation of a negative obligation (gossip, betrayal, taboo-breaking) has a different phenomenology from violating a positive one (debt default), and the schema captures this structurally.
Every obligation created by a temporal-deontic schema follows the same lifecycle. This is the state machine at the heart of social interaction:

props.title: Obligation State Machine
props.description: An obligation is created when a deontic schema executes. It becomes active during the interval. Then one of four things happens — each defining a different social outcome.

controls.label: What happens to the obligation?
options: DISCHARGED — Bob returns the book (obligation fulfilled)
VIOLATED — deadline passes, Bob keeps it (breach of social contract)
FORGIVEN — Alice says 'keep it' (voluntary destruction of valid claim)
TRANSFERRED — Alice assigns the debt to Carol (obligation reassigned)

children.children: FORGIVE is the most remarkable transition. It's not just 'being nice' — it's the voluntary annihilation of a valid claim. The forgiver COULD demand discharge and instead chooses destruction. This is why forgiveness is considered profound across cultures: it operates at the meta-level, treating one's own rights as objects that can be discarded.
Here's where it gets truly interesting. If we track an agent's pattern of obligation creation, discharge, violation, and forgiveness, familiar personality traits EMERGE as statistical signatures:
Generous
Creates fewer obligations than expected. GIFT > LEND ratio is high. Gives without demanding return.
Greedy
Creates more obligations than warranted. Never gifts, always lends. Maximizes incoming obligations.
Reliable
High discharge rate on own obligations. Does what they said they'd do.
Vindictive
Never forgives. Pursues violations aggressively. Zero forgiveness rate on obligations owed to them.
Grateful
Recognizes GIFTs that could have been LENDs. Acknowledges the absent obligation.
Entitled
Treats LENDs as GIFTs. Denies that obligations exist. High violation rate because they don't believe they owe anything.
Manipulative
High DECEIVE and THREATEN usage. Creates false beliefs and coercive obligations.
Discreet
Honors CONFIDE (negative) obligations. Keeps secrets. Never violates information trust.

Think of each person as a portfolio manager — but instead of stocks, they manage obligations. A generous person runs a 'charity fund' (outflows exceed inflows). A greedy person runs a 'hedge fund' (maximizes returns on every transaction). A forgiving person writes off bad debts voluntarily. A vindictive person pursues every delinquent account to the bitter end. Your personality IS your obligation management policy.

props.title: Dickens's Diff: Character Arcs as Vector Rotations

children.children: The most stunning application: literary character arcs are measurable transformations in deontic trait space. Let's analyze the most famous redemption arc in English literature.

props.title: A Christmas Carol — The Deontic Diff

children.children: SCROOGE PRE-GHOSTS: Lends to everyone, never gifts. Never forgives debts. Creates obligations on others, accepts none himself. Threatens employees.
Profile: { greedy: 1.0, miserly: 1.0, vindictive: 1.0, domineering: 0.8 }
Archetype: THE MISER
SCROOGE POST-GHOSTS: Gifts instead of lending. Forgives Bob's debt. Raises Bob's salary (better HIRE terms). Donates to charity.
Profile: { generous: 1.0, charitable: 1.0, forgiving: 0.5 }
Archetype: THE REDEEMED
THE DIFF: greedy → 0 (-1.0), vindictive → 0 (-1.0), domineering → 0 (-0.8), generous → 1.0 (+1.0), charitable → 1.0 (+1.0), forgiving → 0.5 (+0.5)

This is not metaphor. These are computable quantities derived from the sequence of schema executions in the narrative. Dickens's entire novel is a rotation in obligation space — a vector that was pointing toward maximum extraction rotates to point toward maximum generosity.

props.title: Build a Character's Deontic Profile
props.description: Choose actions for a character and watch their personality traits shift. Each action is a compound operator that creates or resolves obligations.

controls.label: Character performs:
options: GIFT — Give with no strings attached (+generous)
LEND — Transfer with obligation to return (+domineering as creditor)
PROMISE and KEEP — Make and fulfill commitment (+reliable)
PROMISE and BREAK — Make and violate commitment (+unreliable)
CONFIDE and HONOR — Keep a secret (+discreet)
CONFIDE and BETRAY — Reveal a secret (+gossipy)

children.children: Valjean's arc: STEAL (Miser-adjacent) → GIFT/FORGIVE (Saint). The turning point is the bishop's candlesticks — a GIFT where a THREATEN was expected. The bishop executed FORGIVE on Valjean's STEAL obligation, which was so unexpected that it restructured Valjean's entire obligation policy. One act of forgiveness rewrote a personality.

Javert is perfectly RELIABLE but totally VINDICTIVE — 100% discharge rate, 0% forgiveness. His crisis occurs when Valjean (a violator of obligations) acts with greater moral authority than the obligation system Javert serves. The system is self-consistent but WRONG. Javert's suicide is the collapse of a deontic profile that cannot accommodate forgiveness. His personality literally has no state transition for it.

Raskolnikov's arc is the INVERSE of Scrooge's: from ENTITLED (denies obligations exist — believes he is above social contracts) to ACCEPTING (voluntarily submits to the obligation system by confessing). His crime is a denial of the deontic world's legitimacy. His redemption is re-entering it.
How do you recognize when a verb is a compound schema? Five heuristics:
1. Reversal Test
Does the action imply its own undoing? Same object, swapped roles, temporal gap → LEND family. Examples: lend/return, kidnap/release, borrow/repay.
2. Obligation Shadow Test
Does the action create an expectation beyond the immediate event? One actual step + deontic step → PROMISE family. Examples: hire, subscribe, commission.
3. Perspective Swap Test
Is there a word for the same structure from the other side? Isomorphic decomposition, swapped roles → paired schemas. Examples: lend/borrow, teach/learn, sell/buy.
4. Conditional Branch Test
Does the action depend on an uncertain outcome? Deontic steps with conditions → BET family. Examples: bet, insure, guarantee.
5. Meta-Operation Test
Does the action operate on obligations themselves? Object = obligation → FORGIVE family. Examples: forgive, delegate, default, renegotiate.

props.question: The verb 'SUBSCRIBE' (e.g., subscribing to a magazine) decomposes into which type of compound schema?
options: Type II — Temporal-deontic with periodic payment obligation (like RENT)
props.explanation: SUBSCRIBE is a periodic temporal-deontic schema: the publisher delivers content (ATRANS_actual), and the subscriber is obligated to pay periodically (ATRANS_deontic, periodic). It's structurally similar to RENT — ongoing access in exchange for recurring obligation.

children.children: This framework reveals something surprising: a huge fraction of human social interaction is deontic world management. We spend our lives creating, tracking, discharging, violating, and forgiving obligations. The actual-world actions are almost trivial; the interesting structure lives in the modal world of what-should-be.

Physical events are the tip of the iceberg — visible above the waterline. Below the surface, invisible but massive, is the deontic structure: who owes what to whom, what's expected, what's been forgiven. When you say someone 'lent' you something, 90% of what you've communicated is underwater — the obligation, the timeline, the expectation of return. The word 'lent' is a compressed pointer to this entire submerged structure.

props.question: Why is FORGIVE considered a 'meta-operator' rather than a regular compound operator?
options: Because it's very rare in everyday language
Because it acts on OBLIGATIONS (deontic objects) rather than physical objects
Because it requires two agents instead of one
Because it has no Schankian primitive decomposition
props.explanation: FORGIVE is a meta-operator because its 'object' is an obligation itself, not a physical or abstract entity. Regular operators act on things in the world (transfer a book, communicate information). FORGIVE acts on the deontic world — it destroys a valid claim. It's an operator in the meta-language of obligations.

props.question: In deontic terms, what makes STEAL different from BORROW?
options: STEAL has no ATRANS step; BORROW does
STEAL creates the obligation from SOCIETY's perspective; BORROW from the lender's
They have identical decompositions — only intent differs
STEAL is a meta-operator; BORROW is not
props.explanation: Both STEAL and BORROW involve ATRANS (actual) + ATRANS (deontic, reversed). The crucial difference: in BORROW, the obligation is consensual (created by the lender). In STEAL, the obligation is imposed by SOCIETY — the thief didn't agree to return anything, but the social contract says they should. The source of the deontic assertion differs even though the structure is isomorphic.

children.children: The working implementation (compound_operators.py) includes all 18 schemas, obligation lifecycle tracking, a deontic profiler with 24 trait dimensions across 7 axes, literary archetype classification, and character arc comparison. It integrates with the Modal Lambda Knowledge Store for cross-world obligation tracking.
